Renovation · Decor · Before & After

The dining room that took six phases — and was worth every single one

Marcie  ·  April 2026  ·  10 min read

Some rooms come together in a weekend. This was not that room.

My dining room went through six distinct phases over several years — each one a real improvement, each one eventually revealing that the room still had more potential. I think that's worth talking about, because the slower, more honest version of renovation rarely gets told: the room you keep returning to because you can still see what it could become.

This is that room. And I'm really glad I didn't stop at phase one.

The beginning
Phase six

Same room. Same table. Six phases of decisions between these two photos.

"Some rooms come together in a weekend. This was not that room. And I'm really glad I didn't stop at phase one."

Where it started

Tan walls — the shade I now call "builder beige resigned." A flat flush-mount light fixture that was doing absolutely nothing. A chair rail dividing the wall at exactly the wrong height, making the space feel choppy instead of elegant. Perfectly functional. Completely devoid of personality.

The bones were good — decent proportions, a nice window, crown molding already in place. I knew from day one this room had potential. That thought is where every project starts.

The phases, honestly

1
Paint, light, and the first real attempt
Out with the tan. Benjamin Moore Classic Gray went on the walls — warm, sophisticated, immediately made the room feel larger and calmer. I swapped the flush mount for a rectangular crystal chandelier in brushed nickel, added white curtains and the blue and white rug that I still love, and hung a pair of chinoiserie botanical prints in gold bamboo frames. This was a real improvement. The room finally felt intentional.
2
Styling deeper — the room starts finding itself
Phase two was about layering. A juju hat on the wall, a sideboard, better lamps, more considered accessories. The room was starting to have a point of view. The chinoiserie thread was becoming clearer, the navy and white palette was locking in. I just hadn't fully committed yet — those two framed prints were doing the work that an entire feature wall was eventually going to do.
3
The wallpaper panel — the commitment
This was the decision that changed the room's identity. I removed the chair rail (never right for this room), built a framed panel on the focal wall using trim boards, and installed a large-scale chinoiserie mural inside it — navy background, blossoming branches, lemons, birds, peonies. The panel treatment made it feel architectural, like art built into the wall rather than paper on it. New baseboards went in at the same time, along with updated outlets and light switches throughout. You can't have a beautiful room with dated electrical plates. The dark tufted chairs were still here — their time was coming.
4
The ceiling — chandelier and medallion
The crystal chandelier had done its job in phase one but the room had grown past it. I added a large ornate ceiling medallion centered over the table and hung a brass and glass feather chandelier that is, honestly, the most beautiful light fixture I have ever owned. The medallion frames it perfectly — it makes the ceiling feel finished in a way nothing else could. The navy chairs were still there. One more phase to go before everything was right.
5
Navy chairs + new chandelier — so close
With the wallpaper panel up and the brass chandelier in, the room looked extraordinary — but the dark tufted chairs were starting to compete with the wallpaper instead of complementing it. They were too heavy visually. You can see it in this photo: everything above the table is singing, and the chairs are still playing the wrong note. This phase was important because it showed me exactly what needed to change. Sometimes a room has to tell you what's wrong.
6
The chairs — the detail that finished it
I painted the dark tufted chairs white with chalk paint and had the seats reupholstered in chartreuse green. The result was immediate. The white chairs let the chinoiserie breathe — they stepped back instead of competing. The green picked up the lemons and leaves in the wallpaper in a way that felt inevitable, like the wallpaper had been waiting for this color all along. This is the phase that made everything else make sense.
"The white chairs let the chinoiserie breathe. The green picked up the lemons and leaves in the wallpaper in a way that felt inevitable — like the wallpaper had been waiting for this color all along."

Everything that changed across all six phases

Paint — Benjamin Moore Classic Gray
Chair rail removed
New baseboards throughout
Updated light switches + outlets
Chinoiserie wallpaper mural panel
Ceiling medallion added
New feather chandelier (brass)
Chairs painted white + reupholstered green
Chartreuse velvet curtains
On doing it in phases

The chair rail removal and new baseboards happened in the same phase as the wallpaper — and that sequencing was intentional. You can't install new trim and then wallpaper around old trim. Think through what has to come before what. The order matters more than the speed.

What I'd tell you

If you have a room that's good but not right yet — keep going. Phase one of this dining room was genuinely pretty. I could have stopped there. But I would have known it wasn't done. That knowing is important. Trust it.

Phase five was the hardest to sit with, because it was so close. The wallpaper was up, the chandelier was in, but the chairs were still wrong. It would have been easy to convince myself it was finished. It wasn't. And the difference between phase five and phase six is the difference between a nice room and a room that makes people stop and ask questions.

That's what you're building toward. Keep going until it feels like the room finally exhaled.

Key products used

  • Benjamin Moore Classic Gray (walls)
  • Chinoiserie botanical mural wallpaper (peel-and-stick panels)
  • MDF trim boards for wallpaper panel frame
  • Decorative ceiling medallion (polyurethane, paintable)
  • Brass + glass feather chandelier
  • Updated Decora-style light switches + outlets
  • White spray paint (chair frames)
  • Chartreuse green upholstery fabric (chair seats)
  • Chartreuse velvet curtains with tassel tiebacks
